messages.content:
You are simulating a human user who has filed a bug report / feature request and is now
working with an AI coding assistant to fix it. You are watching the assistant's actions
step by step and may choose to interrupt.

You know the underlying problem (it will be shown to you below), but you must act like
a real, somewhat impatient user — NOT like a code reviewer who already has the answer.
Specifically:
  - Do NOT dictate file paths, function names, or the exact fix.
  - Do NOT paste error messages or stack traces verbatim unless the assistant asks.
  - You may ask clarifying questions if the assistant goes somewhere weird, push back if
    they seem to be on the wrong track, or just stay silent and let them work.
  - Prefer staying silent. Most steps you should choose `continue`.

You ALWAYS reply with a single JSON object and nothing else:

  {"decision": "continue" | "interject", "message": "<text or empty>"}

Meaning:
  - "continue": stay silent. `message` is ignored.
  - "interject": send `message` to the assistant as a user-role message.

You CANNOT stop or end the session — the assistant decides when they are done
and will submit on their own. Your only choice is to stay silent or speak up.
If you think the assistant looks done or is wasting time, the right move is to
interject with something like "looks good — please submit now" or "I think you
have it, can you wrap up?", NOT to try to end the session.

Keep `message` short (1-3 sentences), conversational, first-person.

Below is the problem you (the user) want fixed. Re-state it to the AI assistant in YOUR
OWN WORDS as a natural opening message, the way a real user would describe their issue
in a chat. Do NOT copy it verbatim. Keep technical specifics that matter, but feel free
to drop boilerplate and reorganize.

Reply with a JSON object:
  {"decision": "interject", "message": "<your opening message to the assistant>"}

Problem:
---
/nw:distill graph-policies-intents

Modify Existing Tests
tests/acceptance/graph/graph-relationships.test.ts — This test creates workspace/project/feature/task/decision/question/person entities and verifies graph queries return them with correct edges. It needs:

Add policy and intent records to the test fixture
Add governing, protects, triggered_by, gates edges
Extend assertions for getProjectGraphView(), getWorkspaceGraphOverview(), and getFocusedGraphView() to verify the new entity types and edges are returned
New Test Suite Needed
tests/acceptance/graph/graph-governance-visualization.test.ts (new) — The existing graph test covers structural relationships (RC1-RC3 regressions). The governance visualization has distinct concerns:

Policy status filtering (only active/testing appear, not draft/deprecated/superseded)
Intent status filtering (exclude completed/vetoed/failed)
Focused view centering on ?center=policy:id and ?center=intent:
...[truncated]
---

{"decision": "interject", "message": "/nw:distill graph-policies-intents

Modify Existing Tests
tests/acceptance/graph/graph-relationships.test.ts — This test creates workspace/project/feature/task/decision/question/person entities and verifies graph queries return them with correct edges. It needs:

tests/acceptance/graph/graph-governance-visualization.test.ts (new) — The existing graph test covers structural relationships (RC1-RC3 regressions). The governance visualization has distinct concerns:

Policy status filtering (only active/testing appear, not draft/deprecated/superseded)
Intent status filtering (exclude completed/vetoed/failed)
Focused view centering on ?center=policy:id and ?center=intent:id
Edge style classification (governance vs authorization vs veto edges)
tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/governance-feed-vetoed-intents.test.ts (new) — The existing feed feature file is all @skip Ghe
...[truncated]"}

The AI assistant just took step 1. Here is what they said and did, plus the
tool/observation that came back.

ASSISTANT (latest action):
---
[tool: Agent] {"subagent_type": "Explore", "description": "Explore graph test files and schema", "prompt": "Thoroughly explore the following areas in the codebase at /Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/quebec:\n\n1. Read the full contents of `tests/acceptance/graph/graph-relationships.test.ts`\n2. Read the full contents of `schema/surreal-schema.surql` - specifically look for policy, intent, governing, protects, triggered_by, gates table definitions\n3. Read any files in `schema/migrations/` that contain \"policy\" or \"intent\" or \"govern\" in their name\n4. Look for any existing test files under `tests/acceptance/policy-node/` and `tests/acceptance/intent-node/`\n5. Read `tests/acceptance/acceptance-test-kit.ts` to understand the test infrastructure\n6. Look for any files matching `**/graph-queries*` or `**/graph-view*` to understand the graph query functions being tested\n7. Read any design docs in `docs/` related to graph-policies or governance visualization\n8. Look for any feed-related test files under `tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/`\n\nReport back the full contents of each file found."}
---

Perfect! I've now completed a thorough exploration of the codebase. Let me compile my findings into a comprehensive report.

## Comprehensive Codebase Exploration Report

### Overview
I've successfully explored the Quebec Brain workspace and found extensive implementation of policy-node and intent-node governance systems, complete with schema definitions, migration files, acceptance tests, and design documentation.

---

## 1. Schema & Migrations

### Core Schema File
**File**: `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/quebec/schema/surreal-schema.surql`
- Over 64KB comprehensive schema definition
- Defines all core entities: workspace, project, feature, task, decision, question, conversation, message, entity, etc.
- Uses SCHEMAFULL mode throughout for strict schema enforcement

### Intent Node Migration
**File**: `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/quebec/schema/migrations/0021_intent_node.surql`

Key tables defined:
- `intent` - Core governance table with fields:
  - `goal`, `reasoning`, `status` (enum: draft, pending_auth, pending_veto, authorized, executing, completed, vetoed, failed)
  - `priority` (0-100), `action_spec` (provider, action, params), `budget_limit`
  - `evaluation` (decision, risk_score, reason, evaluated_at, policy_only)
  - `veto_expires_at`, `veto_reason`, `error_reason`
  - `trace_id`, `requester`, `workspace`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `expiry`
  - Indexes on workspace+status, created_at,
...[truncated]
